coredata.dc:title: Multi-talented applications for cell imaging, tumor cells recognition, patterning, staining and temperature sensing by using egg white-encapsulated gold nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Lu
Zhao, Wenjing
Li, Lin
Tong, Yaoli
Peng, Guanlin
Li, Yingqi
coredata.dc:description: We herein provide a facile and green strategy for the fabrication of red fluorescence gold nanoclusters (AuNCs@ew) with low toxicity, good stability and excellent fluorescence (λem =650nm, λex =320nm) by mixing aqueous tetrachloroauric acid with diluted egg white under alkaline environment using microwave-assisted method. Confocal fluorescence imaging showed that the AuNCs@ew can enter living cells and distribute in the cytoplasm. In addition, the fluorescence of AuNCs@ew is sensitive to the response of Cu2+ and Hg2+ with a detection limit of 14.48nM and 0.81nM, respectively. Interestingly, we found that the fluorescent signal of the quenched AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) system could be recovered in the presence of the glutathione (GSH) in Tris-HCl medium (pH 6.5). This assay displayed a rapid response and highly selectivity toward GSH with a detection limit of 91.9nM. More excitingly, the fluorescent signal of the AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) system (OFF) could be activated in living tumor cells (ON) such as HepG2 cells rather than in living normal cells (HT22 cells, OFF) without any exogenous GSH. The result showed that the system can be successfully applied for tumor cells recognition, which implied its great potential application in early prevention of cancer. Furthermore, the AuNCs@ew can also be used for patterning, staining and temperature sensing. We propose that the highly fluorescent AuNCs@ew can be eco-friendly and potentially large-scale development for versatile applications and so merits further study.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Egg white
Gold nanoclusters
Tumor recognition
Patterning
Staining
Temperature sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of fluorescent ew-capped AuNCs
Patterning and staining from AuNCs@ew solution
Fluorescence restoration based on the quenched AuNCs@ew-Hg2+ system by GSH
AuNCs@ew for patterning, staining and temperature sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the response of the AuNCs@ew−Hg(II) system to GSH.

Characterization of AuNCs@ew. (A) FTIR spectra of chicken egg white (black) and AuNCs@ew (blue) by microwave method. (B) XRD patterns. (C) TEM image. (D) The size distribution. (E) UV–vis absorption (red line), fluorescence emission (black line) spectra. Inset: the photographs of the AuNCs@ew under visible light (left) and 365nm UV light irradiation (right). (F) XPS spectrum of Au 4f. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Relative fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ew (F/F0, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of AuNCs@ew in the presence and absence of metal ions, respectively) (λex =365nm, λem =650nm) in the presence of various metal ions. Inset: digital photographs of aqueous AuNCs@ew solutions (0.2mM) in the presence of 1.0mM of different metal ions under UV illumination. (B) (a) Fluorescence emission spectra of AuNCs@ew with the addition of different Cu2+ concentrations increasing from 0 to 50,000nM (excitation at 320nm). (b) Plot of the relative fluorescence intensity versus the Cu2+ concentration. (C) (a) Fluorescence emission spectra of AuNCs@ew with the addition of different Hg2+ concentrations increasing from 0 to 10,000nM (excitation at 320nm). (b) Plot of the relative fluorescence intensity versus the Hg2+ concentration.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of AuNCs@ew in the presence of different concentrations of GSH. (B) GSH concentration-dependent change of fluorescence intensity (λmax =650nm). Inset: Linear range between the change of fluorescence intensity (ΔI/I0) at 650nm and the GSH concentration (5–35μM). The optimal concentration of Hg2+ is 10μM.
Fluorescence responses of the AuNCs@ew–Hg(II) system to GSH(50μM) and 10 other biological molecules (5-fold concentration of GSH).

Living cell imaging of AuNCs@ew. Fluorescence confocal images and bright field of cells after incubation with AuNCs@ew for 1.0h. (A) HeLa cells; (B) HepG2 cells. The images in the left column show the red fluorescence of the AuNCs@ew; the middle column shows the DIC images of cells in bright field; and the right column shows the merge of the two previous images. Scale bar: 10μm and 20μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence confocal images of HepG2 cells after incubation with AuNCs@ew–Hg(II) for 0, 12, 15, 21, 30 and 45min. (B) Fluorescence confocal images of normal cells (HT22 cells) after incubation with AuNCs@ew–Hg(II) for 1.0h. The images in the left column show the fluorescence of the AuNCs@ew added to HT22 cells; the middle column shows the DIC images of cells in bright field; and the right column shows the merge of the two previous images. Scale bar: 20μm.

AuNCs@ew for patterning, staining and temperature sensing. (A) Different photographs of the fluorescence patterns under 365nm excitation. (B) Different images of filter papers stained by AuNCs@ew under 365nm excitation. (C) AuNCs@ew for temperature sensing. (a) The fluorescence intensities of AuNCs@ew with temperature changing from 0 to 65°C. (b) The fluorescence intensities of AuNCs@ew when the temperature returned to room temperature.

head.ce:title: Multi-talented applications for cell imaging, tumor cells recognition, patterning, staining and temperature sensing by using egg white-encapsulated gold nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Molecular Engineering of Ministry of Education, Institute of Molecular Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Molecular Engineering of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Translational Medicine Center, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We herein provide a facile and green strategy for the fabrication of red fluorescence gold nanoclusters (AuNCs@ew) with low toxicity, good stability and excellent fluorescence (λem =650nm, λex =320nm) by mixing aqueous tetrachloroauric acid with diluted egg white under alkaline environment using microwave-assisted method. Confocal fluorescence imaging showed that the AuNCs@ew can enter living cells and distribute in the cytoplasm. In addition, the fluorescence of AuNCs@ew is sensitive to the response of Cu2+ and Hg2+ with a detection limit of 14.48nM and 0.81nM, respectively. Interestingly, we found that the fluorescent signal of the quenched AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) system could be recovered in the presence of the glutathione (GSH) in Tris-HCl medium (pH 6.5). This assay displayed a rapid response and highly selectivity toward GSH with a detection limit of 91.9nM. More excitingly, the fluorescent signal of the AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) system (OFF) could be activated in living tumor cells (ON) such as HepG2 cells rather than in living normal cells (HT22 cells, OFF) without any exogenous GSH. The result showed that the system can be successfully applied for tumor cells recognition, which implied its great potential application in early prevention of cancer. Furthermore, the AuNCs@ew can also be used for patterning, staining and temperature sensing. We propose that the highly fluorescent AuNCs@ew can be eco-friendly and potentially large-scale development for versatile applications and so merits further study.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials, such as silica dots, semiconductor quantum dots, carbon dots and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), have exhibited size-dependent tunable electronic, optical and mechanical properties, so they have drawn widespread attention in many fields including electronics, photonics, energy, catalysis, and medicine [1]. Among fluorescent nanomaterials, AuNCs becomes an object of keen scientific interest in recent years, owing to its advantages of long lifetime, large Stokes shift, biocompatibility and fluorescence from the visible to near-infrared (NIR) region [2–4]. Therefore, the excellent properties contribute the AuNCs for various applications, including solar energy harvesting, sensing, catalysis, bioimaging, drug delivery and photothermal cancer therapy [5]. Recently, water-soluble fluorescence AuNCs has been synthesized by various approaches. Approaches based on chemical reduction have been applied to prepare AuNCs in the presence of peptides [6] or proteins [7], where the proteins can act as both reducing and capping agents. Since the AuNCs was synthesized using bovine serum albumin by Xie et al. [8], inspired by this discovery, a number of researches have been carried out exploring new proteins (e.g. lysozyme [9], insulin [10] and transferrin [11]) for the synthesis of fluorescent gold nanoclusters. Meanwhile, their biosensing applications have already received great attention.

One of the most noteworthy applications of various probes is toward glutathione (GSH) selectivity and sensitivity, which is involved in many biological processes. For example, GSH plays the essential roles in the body's important antioxidant and free radical scavengers [12]. The content changes of GSH are likely a direct result of aging, Parkinson's disease, and the incidence of cancer [13,14]. A number of methods for the detection of GSH have been currently available described, such as capillary electrophoresis, mass spectrometry, high-performance liquid chromatography, and fluorescence spectroscopy [12,15,16]. Compared to the mentioned methods, fluorescence-based strategies get a lot of attention owing to its high sensitivity, simplicity, and short response time. Recently, Yang and co-workers designed a dye BODIPY-based fluorescence sensor to detect GSH in living cells [17]. But most of the dye derivatives are water-insoluble, the dyes often emit fluorescence less than 600nm, and toxicity can not be ignored. In addition, quantum dots have also been used to measure the GSH [18], yet this probe has low sensitivity due to the high probe background signals. Tian et al. built up a BSA-capped AuNCs-Hg(II) system, which applied in GSH sensing in living cells [12]. Similarly, Zhang et al. concluded that the fluorescence intensity of l-histidine modified AuNCs showed difference in tumor cells with different GSH level [19]. Unfortunately, the preparation of above both AuNCs took a long time and the latter produced high fluorescence background signals.

Considering to the proteins being expensive, large-scale development of the application of AuNCs is limited. Recently there have been several studies on the inexpensive chicken egg white as the materials for red fluorescence AuNCs synthesis. For example, Li et al. demonstrated the application of AuNCs@ew for H2O2 sensing [20]. K. Selvaprakash et al. have further characterized the AuNCs@ew well and it was applied to the detection of ATP and PPi [21]. To our knowledge, the deep research on the AuNCs@ew is rare, involving in the optimal preparation conditions and its multi-talented applications. For instance, since most of the gold nanoclusters can be used for the detection of Hg(II), whether it can be made a test strip which is similar to a pH test strip? Since AuNCs-Hg(II) system can be used to detect GSH in living tumor cells, and as well know that GSH level is different between tumor cells and normal cells, whether the AuNCs-Hg(II) system can be used to distinguish between tumor cells and normal cells? In addition, since AuNCs is the water-solubility, luminescence and low toxicity, how can we use it in other ways?

In this work, we attempted to prepare a red fluorescence egg white protein-encapsulated gold nanoclusters (AuNCs@ew, λem =650nm) probe by a green, eco-friendly, simple and rapid process. Due to the advantageous properties of AuNCs@ew, such as low toxicity, good stability and excellent fluorescence, as-synthesized AuNCs@ew has been successfully applied for cell imaging, the applications in a Hg(II) detection test strip, patterning, staining and temperature sensing. More importantly, the AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) system (OFF) was founded to endow good sensitivity and selectivity between the tumor cells (ON) and normal cells (OFF), which showed the potential application in tumor cells recognition and may contribute to the early prevention of cancer.

All the chemicals of the AuNCs synthesis and detection were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Throughout all the experiments doubly distilled deionized water was used and it was prepared using SZ series of automatic water distiller (Shanghai,China). Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) tetrahydrate (HAuCl4∙4H2O) was bought from Tianjin, Fresh chicken eggs were obtained from a supermarket near our university. Glutathione reduced (GSH) was purchased from Shanghai. l-cysteine (Cys) was purchased from Solarbio (China), and tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris) was bought from Tianjin. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was obtained from Beijing Chemicals (Beijing, China). Riboflavin 5′-monophosphate sodium salt hydrate was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Stock solutions (0.01molL−1) of all the cations and biological molecules (Na+, K+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cu2+ and Hg2+; Lysine (Lys), Phenylalanine (PHE), Tyrosine (Tyr), Glycine (Gly), Dithiothreitol (DTT), glucose) were prepared by directly dissolving in doubly-distilled water.

The list equipments and apparatus used are as follows: the synthesis of AuNCs@ew was reacted in Discover SP (CEM, America), the UV–vis absorption spectra was obtained on a Cary 50 Bio UV–vis Spectrophotometer (America). Fluorescence measurements were carried out using a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Australia). The slit wavelengths for excitation and emission were both set as 5nm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained on a JEM-2100 (JEOL Ltd, Tokyo, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded on a FTIR-8400S (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan). The size distribution of AuNCs was recorded on Malvern Nano Particle Sizer (ZETA Sizer, Nano-ZS90, England). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was obtained by using an AXIS ULTRA DLD electron spectrometer (Shimadzu Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data was recorded on Bruker D8 Advance (Germany). The fluorescence lifetime was carried out by using the X Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (England). The relative fluorescence QY (Φ) of the AuNCs was calculated using the equation:
ϕ x
=
ϕ
s t d
F x
A
s t d
η x 2
/ (
F
s t d
A x
η
s t d
2
)
. The optical densities were measured using a Cary 50 Bio UV–vis Spectrophotometer and a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. Riboflavin 5′-monophosphate sodium salt hydrate in water was chosen as a standard with a quantum yield Φstd =26% at 370nm. The absorbances of all the samples in a 1.0cm cuvette were kept under 0.05 at the excitation wavelength to minimize re-absorption effects. The cellular uptake and the localization of materials in the intracellular spaces were visualized via laser confocal scanning microscopy. Images were obtained using a Confocal laser scanning microscope (LSM880+Airyscan, Zeiss).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of fluorescent ew-capped AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: All vessels were washed with aqua regia (HCl/HNO3 (V:V=3:1)) and rinsed with doubly distilled deionized water for experiments. In a typical microwave-assisted synthesis experiment, egg white was separated carefully from the whole egg. 4.0mL egg white was taken out and was diluted to 100mL by doubly-distilled water, stirred and centrifuged. HAuCl4 (2.5×10−3 M, 1.0mL) was added to a 2mL resultant clear egg white solution. The mixture was thoroughly mixed by shaking for 5min, followed by 40μL of 0.1M NaOH solution with continuous shaking for 2min. Then the solution was added in the microwave vessel and was placed in reaction chamber and sealed. The microwave apparatus was set at 150W, 90°C, and 250Psi and then was turned on to react for 30min with high stirring rate. The mixture was cooled to room temperature in the equipment quickly and it changed from turbid light yellow to clear yellow. Experiments were carried out in different reaction temperature, time and concentration to determine the optimum conditions. Without any megascopic impurities after centrifugation, the solution was stored at 4°C for further use.

In a typical test, 200μL of the as-prepared ew-capped AuNCs (AuNCs@ew) and 800μL of buffer solution of pH 6.5 (0.1M Tris-HCl) were added to 1.0mL doubly-distilled water containing various metal ions, such as Na+, K+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cu2+ and Hg2+, respectively. In order to evaluate AuNCs@ew on the sensitivity and selectivity of ions, after two minutes of mixing, the fluorescence spectra from 520nm to 850nm were recorded with excitation at 320nm.

For the selectivity study about GSH detection assay, an aliquot of the stock solutions samples was prepared in double-distilled water including some common coexisting biological molecules (Cys), some amino acids (Lys, PHE, Tyr, Gly), small molecules (including DTT and glucose) and metal ions, such as Na+, K+ and Ca2+. 10μL of above-mentioned substances were added to 200μL of the AuNCs@ew solution containing 10μL Hg2+ (10μM), which were adjusted to 2mL with double-distilled water. In order to evaluate AuNCs@ew on the sensitivity and selectivity of the above mentioned samples, after two minutes of mixing, fluorescence spectra were recorded from 520nm to 850nm with excitation at 320nm.

Firstly, 2μM of Hg2+ ions was titrated into the as-prepared AuNCs@ew solution in a Tris-HCl buffer (0.01M, pH 6.5), and the resulting solution is incubated at room temperature for 2min to ensure complete reaction. Secondly, the above solution was added by different concentrations of GSH or Cys to retain 1.0min every time, and fluorescence spectra were recorded with excitation wavelength of 320nm.

The cellular imaging of AuNCs@ew was performed on the confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). The experimental procedures were as follows: HepG2 cells and HeLa cells were seeded into 35mm dish with 20mm bottom of the glass for confocal imaging at a density of 1×105 cells per dish in 1.0mL of DMEM with 10% serum and incubated for 20h, followed by removing the medium and substituting for 1.0mL fresh DMEM with 10% serum medium containing AuNCs@ew at a final AuNCs@ew concentration of 2.0mg/mL. The cells were incubated at 37°C for1.0h. Subsequently, the cells were washed thrice with ice-cold PBS (pH 7.4). Finally, the cells were observed and the images were recorded with a LSM880.

Given the AuNCs@ew probe imaging in living cells and the sensitivity of AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) quenching systems for GSH, we devised a simple way to quickly detect tumor cells. Specifically, HepG2 cells were chosen as a representative of tumor cells with high GSH level, and HT22 cells were on behalf of normal cells with low GSH level. The cells were seeded at a density of 1.0×105 cells per dish onto a 35mm culture dish with the cover glass on the bottom. The cells were cultured for 20h before treatment. Then the cells were washed twice with PBS (pH 7.4). Next the AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) system (OFF) was added to the culture dish, a photograph was taken every three minutes by the CLSM for 45min for HepG2 cells. A total of 15 pieces of photographs were captured. In the experiment, the normal cells (HT22 cells) were chosen as a comparison. After cultured by AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) system for 1h, the cells were observed and the images were recorded with a LSM880.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Patterning and staining from AuNCs@ew solution
ce:section.ce:para: AuNCs@ew stoke solution (0.25mgmL−1) was filled into the pen for drawing familiar fluorescence patterns, and the filter papers were stained using the AuNCs@ew solution (0.25mgmL−1) at room temperature. After stoving in drying case, photographs were taken using a camera via an ultra-violet analysator (excitation: 365nm).

Typically, 2mL of AuNCs@ew stock solution was introduced into a quartz cuvette, remained for 10min after setting the temperature from 0 to 65°C, and recorded the corresponding fluorescence spectra. When it returned to room temperature, the fluorescence intensity was measured at that time.

Compared with traditional thermal techniques, microwave energy is frequently utilized to provide rapid reaction and homogeneous thermal efficiency to accelerate the synthesis of nanomaterials [22–24]. So a microwave-assisted method was introduced in the experiment. And diluted egg white and tetrachloroauric acid served as raw materials to synthesize AuNCs@ew by one-pot reaction. The contents of diluted egg white and tetrachloroauric acid, pH of the solution, as well as reaction temperature and time were investigated as important parameters for controlling the size, structure, oxidation state, and surface properties of AuNCs@ew. As shown in Fig. S1, the AuNCs@ew was obtained according to the optimal conditions (30min, 90°C, pH 11 and the volume ratio of 1∶2 for tetrachloroauric acid and diluted egg white). In these conditions, we think that alkaline reaction condition is necessary, because the tyrosine residue (the enol structure) from egg white proteins can act as reducing agent under this condition, and breaking disulfide bond from egg white proteins to form mercapto and further to form AuS bond (Au(III) is oxidized to form Au (0) and Au (I), which is detected by XPS in Fig. 1 F) to stabilize gold nanoclusters.

AuNCs@ew was synthesized according to above optimal conditions. The AuNCs@ew is highly water-soluble due to the existence of egg white proteins on its surface, which can be proved from the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1A). As shown in Fig. 1A, in free egg white (black profile), the peaks around 3200–3500cm−1 are attributed to OH and NH stretching vibration, while the peaks become a wider peak at about 3420cm−1 primarily attributed to OH stretching vibration in the FTIR spectrum of the AuNCs@ew (blue profile). Asymmetric stretching vibrations of CO and NH are at 1651cm−1, and 1562cm−1, respectively, for both samples. The most obvious difference in the FTIR spectrum is the appearance of the new peak at 848cm−1 for AuNCs@ew, which may belong to the unsaturated carbon-carbon double bonds similar to the quinone. Such a result is consistent with our speculation that the tyrosine residue (the enol structure) from egg white proteins acted as reducing agent under alkaline condition may be oxidized to the quinone structure by HAuCl4. Furthermore, Fig. 1B demonstrated the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of AuNCs@ew. The XRD analysis showed that the sharp peaks at 31.6° and 45.4°correspond to lattice planes (111) and (200) of AuNCs@ew, respectively. And the other two characteristic peaks marked by their diffraction indices ((220), and (311)) revealed that the resultant nanoclusters were in the face centered cubic (fcc) structure [25,26].

The shape and size of the AuNCs@ew were as well examined through transmission electron microscope (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) technology. The TEM image (Fig. 1C) indicates that the AuNCs@ew were spherical, well dispersed. And based on DLS results, the average size of the AuNCs@ew is about 4–6nm (Fig. 1D). The UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of the synthesized AuNCs@ew were collected in Fig. 1E. One can see that there was absorption from 200nm to 500nm. Under the 320nm excitation, the sample generated the maximum fluorescence emission peak at about 650nm, which is well separated from the spectral region where endogenous fluorescence occurs. The inset displayed the corresponding photographs of generated AuNCs@ew taken under UV light (λmax =365nm) (right) and daylight (left). The AuNCs@ew has bright red fluorescence under 365nm UV light irradiation, and it has pale yellow color under daylight. Compared with organic fluorophores, the AuNCs@ew shows a large Stokes shift (Fig. S2A), which prevents crosstalk between the emission and excitation signals, which implied that it could be an excellent fluorescent probe for biological sensing and imaging. The quantum yield (QY) of the fluorescent AuNCs@ew is determined to be 6.8% by using Riboflavin 5′-monophosphate sodium salt hydrate (QY=26%) as a reference. The fluorescence lifetimes of the AuNCs@ew showed two distinct decay times of 1.26 and 4.09ns, respectively (Fig. S2B). This may indicate that there are fast and slow electron transfer processes. In addition, the element valence of the AuNCs@ew was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1F, the binding energy of Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2 for AuNCs@ew was 87.6eV and 84.0eV, respectively, which corresponds to Au (I)-thiolate complexes and Au (0) film in the AuNCs@ew. The result was consistent with the reported work [27]. The as-prepared AuNCs@ew is very stable without any precipitations at 4°C and the fluorescence intensity remains unchanged even for six months (Fig. S2C), that facilitates their further applications in biomarkers and images, etc. These features open up a broad way toward a promising fluorescent nanoprobe for bioimaging applications.

To evaluate the selectivity of the probe for metal ions, we investigated its fluorescence response toward 15 kinds of common cations (1.0μM), including Na+, K+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cu2+ and Hg2+. We found that only Cu2+ and Hg2+ could lead to an obvious and immediate decrease in fluorescence intensity, as displayed in Fig. 2 A. It showed that the fluorescence of AuNCs@ew can be effectively quenched in the presence of Cu2+ and Hg2+.

In addition, Fig. 2B–C displayed the fluorescence spectra of AuNCs@ew with different concentrations of Cu2+ and Hg2+, where they coordinate with AuNCs@ew and reach equilibrium at approximately 2min, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2B–C, the fluorescence intensity was gradually decreased with increasing the Cu2+/Hg2+ concentration. Notably, Hg2+ can completely quench the fluorescence, while the fluorescence cannot be completely quenched even the addition of excess amount of Cu2+. A correlation coefficient square of 0.984 was obtained based on the quenching effects (I/I0, where I and I0 are the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ew in the presence and absence of Cu2+/Hg2+ at 650nm, respectively) when the quencher concentration of Cu2+ was over the range from 0 to 5000nM in Fig. 2B(b). The detection limit is calculated to be 14.48nM using 3σ/S method, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot [28]. Similarly, Hg2+ ions can be detected over the excellent linear concentrations ranging from 0 to 5000nM (R2 =0.994) in Fig. 2C(b) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.81nM. Such a result manifested that AuNCs@ew could be an ideal candidate for sensitive detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+. As expected, the AuNCs@ew can be prepared as a Hg(II) detection test strip with visualization similar to a pH test strip (Fig. S3).

Then what are the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of AuNCs@ew caused by Cu2+ and Hg2+? Next, this problem is discussed. The quenching ability of Cu2+ ions toward the AuNCs@ew was speculated as follows: (1) Cu2+ ions might interact with histidine and carboxyl groups of the protein on the surface of AuNCs@ew to generate a protein−Cu2+ complex, which induce the decrease of electron density on the surface of the AuNCs@ew [21]; (2) The fluorescence can not be completely quenched, and it may indicate that Cu2+ can not break the AuS binding, which maintains stability of gold nanoclusters. As for Hg2+ ions, it is significantly quenched through (1) strong and specific interactions of some Hg2+ ions and Au+ ions present on the surface by metallophilic bonding (Hg2+−Au+) of their 5d10 centers [8]; (2) According to the phenomenon of precipitation observed by naked eyes, we supposed that Hg2+ ions break the AuS binding and form the precipitation of Hg-S bond consequently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence restoration based on the quenched AuNCs@ew-Hg2+ system by GSH
ce:section.ce:para: Due to abnormal levels of GSH associated with numerous clinical diseases [12] and the quenched AuNCs@ew-Hg2+ system, we wonder if the AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) as a sensing probe can demonstrate a response to the GSH. To test our hypothesis, we first investigated if treatment of the AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) system with GSH resulted in fluorescence activation. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence signal of AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) was gradually restored with the concentration of GSH increased and the restored fluorescence was obviously dependent on the amount of GSH. When the concentration of GSH was increased to 45μM, no further restoring of fluorescence can be observed, which showed that the sensing response has reached the maximum. The change of fluorescence intensity (ΔI/I0) at 650nm was plotted as a function of GSH concentration in Fig. 3B. The ΔI/I0 values were measured over the GSH concentration range of 0–50μM and they followed an equation from GSH concentrations of 5–150μM: ΔI/I0 =−1.48+0.50×[GSH] (R2 =0.9922), ΔI=I−I0, where I0 and I refer to the fluorescence intensities of AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) (λem =650nm) in the absence and presence of GSH, respectively. The detection limit is calculated to be 91.9nM using 3σ/S rule. In comparison to other fluorescence probes (Table S1) [15,29–32], our probe bears the advantages, such as the simplicity in operation, the faster response speed, NIR emission, and large Stokes shift. Therefore, it should be one of the most sensitive NIR “turn-on” sensors for GSH detection compared to previous methods.

We believe this response of GSH based on AuNCs@ew–Hg(II) system is that upon the addition of GSH, Hg2+ combined strongly with GSH and released from the AuNCs@ew, which made fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ew restore, which is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

To elucidate the mechanism, firstly the effect of GSH on AuNCs@ew was performed, where negligible changes of the pristine AuNCs@ew emission were observed even with excess GSH (Fig. S4). This illustrated that the restoration of fluorescence intensity by GSH should be attributed to the interaction of Hg2+ with GSH, instead of Au+. Comparing to other metal ions, Hg2+ has a very high affinity for thiol containing biomolecules, such as GSH, Cys and so on [31]. The formation constant for the Hg2+- thiol bond has been estimated to be as much as 10 orders of magnitude greater than that of the bonding of Hg2+ to other nucleophiles [33]. The constants of three possible Hg−GSH complexes, i.e., Hg(GSH)2, Hg2(GSH)2, and Hg3(GSH)2, were reported to be 9.0×1040, 8.3×1041, and 3.8×1041, respectively[34]. While the constant of AuNCs−Hg(II) was calculated to be 3.51×1018 by the equation of
lg [ (
F 0
− F ) / F ] = lg
K A
+ n lg [ Q ]
(Fig. S5). Hence upon the addition of GSH to AuNCs@ew−Hg(II) solution, the interaction between Hg2+ and GSH is much stronger obviously. However, the fluorescence could not be recovered completely (Fig. S6). Such a phenomenon was related to some Hg2+ existed in the form of Hg2+−Au+ combining.

To evaluate the selectivity of the assay, some common coexisting biological molecules, such as some amino acids, small molecules (including DTT and glucose) and metal ions (Na+, K+ and Ca2+) were investigated to detect fluorescence response. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 , under the same conditions, GSH showed an apparent enhancement of the fluorescence intensity (△I) against all other biological molecules with a 5-fold concentration of GSH, which revealed this probe based on the AuNCs@ew−Hg(II) system has an excellent selectivity and sensitivity for GSH. Although cysteine (Cys) also can cause fluorescence response to this system, Cys content (μM levels) is much lower than that of GSH (mM levels) in biological systems [35–37]. So the AuNCs@ew−Hg(II) system could be applied as a selective nanosensor for GSH detection without any significant interference. The results revealed this fluorescence “switch-on” sensor for GSH detection.

Owing to the good biocompatibility according to MTT assay with the cell viability more than 85% after cell-treated with AuNCs@ew in the concentration range of 0–1.2mg per mL for 24h (Fig. S7), the potential application of AuNCs@ew as a bioimaging was carried out, where HeLa cells and HepG2 cells were incubated in the medium containing 1.2mgmL−1 of AuNCs@ew for 1.0h at 37°C, respectively, and then observed under confocal microscope whether the AuNCs@ew can enter into living cells. As shown in Fig. 5 , the corresponding bright red fluorescence images were observed upon excitation at 405nm. The overlay image illustrates that this material is distributed in the cytoplasm within cells. According to the clear cell morphology, it further demonstrated that the AuNCs@ew has the potential application in cell biolabeling.

Since the intracellular GSH concentration is between 1 and 10mM [30] and AuNCs−Hg(II) system has “switch-on” sensor capability for GSH detection, and to our knowledge, these systems are currently used only in response to tumor cells. However, the difference in the recognition of tumor and normal cells through the fluorescence recovery of AuNCs−Hg(II) by GSH has never been reported.

We hypothesized that upon entering into tumor cells, the activatable probe AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) agent (OFF) will become activated (ON) in the presence of intracellular reduced glutathione (GSH), leading to increased fluorescence. In contrast, it will remain in an OFF state within normal cells, where the GSH levels are low [19]. Based on this idea, we carried out this experiment, where the AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) was added to living HepG2 cells served as tumor cell model and living HT22 cells (mouse hippocampal cells) as normal cell model, respectively, and incubated for 1.0h in a petri dish with a glass bottom without any exogenous GSH. As shown in Fig. 6 A, initially no fluorescence was observed in the HepG2 cells after AuNCs@ew-Hg2+ system was added to the culture dish. It is noteworthy that as time went on, the red fluorescence occurs and becomes stronger gradually, suggesting that the observed increase in fluorescence of the AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) probe was due to intracellular GSH. Moreover, it exhibited much shorter assay time than many previous methods. And the video of the cells fluorescence transformation was recorded in attachment. Interestingly, when AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) system was added to the HT22 cells and the incubation time was prolonged to 1.0h, negligible fluorescence can be seen (Fig. 6B). This is indeed an exciting result. Thus, such a founding indicated that the good reliability of the strategy for the potential application in the preliminary recognition between normal and tumor cells. It may contribute to the early prevention of cancer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AuNCs@ew for patterning, staining and temperature sensing
ce:section.ce:para: At present, single-walled carbon nanotubes, CdTe nanocrystals/polymer composites, fluorescein and carbon dots have been used for patterning [38], while gold nanoclusters applied in patterning has barely reported. Because of the low cytotoxicity, good stability and excellent fluorescence for the as-AuNCs@ew, AuNCs@ew as a fluorescent ink was used for producing various patterns, which shows clear red fluorescence (Fig. 7 A). Obviously, these patterns drawn from the AuNCs@ew solution are visible under 365nm excitation. The “vis-invisible” properties of AuNCs@ew benefit other applications, such as in anti-counterfeit fields as well as in optoelectronic devices [1,39]. In addition, the AuNCs@ew as well serves as a fluorescent dye to be used for staining (Fig. 7B). Filter papers with AuNCs@ew staining present dramatic red fluorescence under 365nm excitation. (Filter papers with AuNCs@ew staining present no red fluorescence under visible light in Fig. S9). Hence, these photographs strongly demonstrate that the fluorescent AuNCs@ew could serve the purpose of staining. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ew displays significantly temperature-dependent feature. As demonstrated in Fig. 7C(a), the fluorescence intensities decrease monotonously by increasing the temperature from 0 to 65°C. More interestingly, when it returned to room temperature, the fluorescence intensity was recovered magically (Fig. 7C(b)). The phenomenon can be interpreted by the enhancement of nonradiative decay, which is consistent with the result reported [40]. When the temperature increases, the nonradiative transition rate and molecule collision frequency increase simultaneously. But the radiative transition rate remains constant, leading to the decrease in emission intensity from the excited state. This extends the application of nanomaterials in terms of temperature sensor.

In summary, an easy, green, and economic route for synthesizing red fluorescence AuNCs@ew was demonstrated in this work by using cheap egg white as both the reductant and stabilizing agent via a one-step microwave-assisted method. The as-prepared AuNCs@ew shows good selectivity and sensing toward Cu2+ and Hg2+. Based on the quenching effect of the AuNCs@ew-Hg(II), a design was established to probe “switch-on” sensor for GSH detection with the detection limit being 91.9nM. In addition, it displayed that AuNCs@ew can act as the cell biolabeling. More interestingly, the AuNCs@ew-Hg(II) system was applied to markedly recognize tumor cells in the light of the difference in the level of GSH between tumor and normal cells. It may contribute to the early prevention of cancer. Moreover, the AuNCs@ew was used as fluorescent inks, fluorescent dyes, which may exploit the wide-ranging anti-counterfeit applications. The nanomaterials can as well be used for temperature sensing. Therefore, we display herein a facile, eco-friendly and potentially large-scale development strategy to produce highly fluorescent AuNCs@ew for multi-talented applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21071091), Shanxi Science and Technology Development Program (Grant No. 20130313021-1 and 201603D321025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.08.147.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lu Tian obtained B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Lvliang College in 2014. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in physical chemistry.
Wenjing Zhao obtained B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Shanxi Datong University in 2014. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in physical chemistry.
Lin Li obtained B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Xinzhou Normal College in 2014. Now she is a second-year doctoral student in Shanxi University and major in inorganic chemistry.

Yaoli Tong is now working as a teacher in Shanxi Medical University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Master's degree in material chemistry from Shanxi University of Science and Technology in 2013.
Guanlin Peng is a senior student in Shanxi University.

Yingqi Li is a professor of Inorganic Chemistry and Physical Chemistry and a PhD Tutor at Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. degree in Shanxi University in 2006. Between 2007 and 2008, she did postdoctoral Academia Sinica in Atomic and Molecular Sciences, Taiwan. She has published extensively in journals such as, Journal of Materials Chemistry, International Journal of Nanomedicine, Integrative Biology, Diamond & Related Materials, Nanomedicine and Therapeutic Delivery etc. Her research interests are in the areas of Nanomaterials drug and chemosensors.
